summary: Gruber challenges the widely-held belief that Mac OS X caused a dramatic surge in Mac users, arguing with quarterly sales data that Apple sold roughly one million Macs per quarter both in the late 1990s and mid-2000s. He contends that Apple's financial struggles in the '90s were caused by inept management, not an inability to sell Macs, and that the Mac never lost its stronghold in creative markets. The real difference with Mac OS X, he argues, is not that it replaced something bad with something great, but that it broadened the Mac's appeal to new audiences like Unix nerds and programmers. He acknowledges that genuine switching is underway but hasn't yet reached a mainstream tipping point. He also pushes back against both the myopia of recent switchers who assume no one cared about Macs before OS X, and the smugness of long-time Mac users who dismiss anyone who wasn't already on board.
key_points: Apple's quarterly Mac sales were roughly one million units in both the late 1990s and mid-2000s, debunking the narrative of a dramatic OS X-driven sales surge
Apple's financial crisis in the '90s was caused by bad management, not declining Mac sales — the creative market stronghold never wavered
Mac OS X's real achievement was broadening appeal rather than replacing something broken — its Unix core attracted nerds while Aqua attracted aesthetes
The Mac was already dominant in web authoring and creative fields in the late '90s, with BBEdit commanding 50% of all web designers
Both recent switchers and long-time Mac users hold myopic views of Mac history that overstate their own group's significance
money_quotes: Apple isn't selling significantly more computers now than they were 10 years ago.
Apple's financial struggles weren't caused by an inability to sell Macs — they were caused by inept management.
The Mac users of today are, by and large, the Mac users of 10 years ago.
The difference between the old Mac OS and Mac OS X isn't that it used to suck but now it's great. The difference is that Mac OS X's appeal is broader; it is good in more ways than the old Mac OS was.
What makes nerds 'nerds' is that they care to an irrational depth about certain specific things; Mac OS X covers more of those things than did Mac OS 9, and so it appeals to many more nerds.
Today, it's hard to find someone serious who thinks Macs 'suck', or that old chestnut that they're 'toy computers'.
You can't appeal to all people all the time, but Mac OS X comes remarkably close. The old Mac OS, as insanely great as it was, did not.
Switching is afoot — but it hasn't yet reached the tipping point where it goes mainstream.
key_insight: Mac OS X didn't rescue Apple from irrelevance — it broadened the Mac's appeal beyond its already-loyal creative base to include the nerd and programmer markets that had previously dismissed it.
